concept.title: Topic-Based Information Architecture for Training
concept.shortdesc: How to design a modular, map-driven information architecture that supports progressive learning paths, role-based content delivery, and AI-powered navigation.

section.title: What are the architecture principles for AI-ready training content?

ol.li: Topics are self-contained: Every topic should be understandable without reading the surrounding content. The chatbot may retrieve a single section from a single topic — that section must make sense on its own.

Maps define context, not topics: The sequence, hierarchy, and relationships between topics are defined in the map, not hardcoded into the topics. This allows the same topic to appear in multiple learning paths.
Progressive depth: Organize content from foundational to advanced, with explicit prerequisite chains so the chatbot can guide users through an appropriate learning path.
Role-based navigation: Use audience metadata and conditional processing to enable filtered views for different roles (technical writer, developer, manager).
Granularity for retrieval: Each topic should cover one focused subject. Each section within a topic should address one specific question. Overly broad topics produce low-quality chunks.

section.title: How should topics be organized in the file system?
section.p: Organize topics by content domain, mapping to the taxonomy categories:
section.codeblock: dita-topics/ ├── getting-started/ # Foundational, beginner content │ ├── dita-intro-guide.dita │ ├── installing-dita-ot.dita │ └── setting-up-authoring-tools.dita ├── core-topics/ # Core subject area content │ ├── dita-authoring.dita │ ├── dita-publishing.dita │ ├── dita-reuse-variants.dita │ └── writing-first-dita-project.dita ├── developer-guides/ # Advanced developer content │ ├── dita-ot-developer-handbook.dita (parent) │ └── devhandbook-topics/ (sub-topics) │ ├── docker-basics.dita │ ├── github-actions-cicd.dita │ └── ...16 files ├── plugins-and-customization/ # Plugin development content │ ├── xslt-customization-tutorial.dita (parent) │ └── xslt-topics/ (sub-topics) │ ├── create-html5-plugin.dita │ ├── debugging-workflow.dita │ └── ...18 files ├── intelligent-content/ # Meta: AI authoring guidelines │ ├── ai-ready-authoring-guide.dita │ ├── schema-org-mapping.dita │ ├── extense-taxonomy.subjectScheme │ ├── chatbot-config.json │ └── chatbot-data/ └── reference/ # Lookup content ├── glossary.dita ├── architecture-diagram.dita └── plugin-reference.dita
section.p: Why this structure matters for AI: The directory organization mirrors the content domain taxonomy, making it easy to maintain classification consistency. The chatbot-data/ directory keeps generated artifacts separate from source content.

section.title: How should the bookmap define learning paths?
section.p: The bookmap creates a progressive learning sequence where each chapter builds on the previous:
section.codeblock:
<bookmap id="extense-training-guide"> <!-- Part 1: Foundation --> <part> <topicmeta><navtitle>Getting Started</navtitle></topicmeta> <chapter href="getting-started/dita-intro-guide.dita"> <topicref href="getting-started/installing-dita-ot.dita"/> <topicref href="getting-started/setting-up-tools.dita"/> </chapter> </part>
<!-- Part 2: Core skills --> <part> <topicmeta><navtitle>Core DITA Skills</navtitle></topicmeta> <chapter href="core-topics/dita-authoring.dita"/> <chapter href="core-topics/dita-reuse-variants.dita"/> <chapter href="core-topics/dita-publishing.dita"/> </part>
<!-- Part 3: Advanced (developer audience) --> <part> <topicmeta><navtitle>Advanced Development</navtitle></topicmeta> <chapter href="developer-guides/dita-ot-developer-handbook.dita"> <topicref href="developer-guides/devhandbook-topics/docker-basics.dita"/> <topicref href="developer-guides/devhandbook-topics/github-actions-cicd.dita"/> <!-- ...more sub-topics --> </chapter> </part> </bookmap>
section.p: The map hierarchy gives the chatbot three critical signals:

ul.li: Sequence: Topics should be read in this order for maximum comprehension
Nesting: Sub-topics are detailed elaborations of their parent topic
Grouping: Parts group topically related content for navigation

section.title: How granular should topics and sections be?
section.p: The right granularity depends on the content purpose:

row.entry: One focused subject per topic. 500–3000 words. 3–8 sections.
Topics that are too long produce too many chunks; too short topics waste metadata overhead.
One question or concept per section. 100–1200 tokens (1–6 paragraphs).
Each section becomes a separately retrievable chunk. It must make sense on its own with the inherited topic metadata.
One sentence, 25–35 words. Describe purpose, not content.
Prepended to every chunk as summary. Vague shortdescs degrade every chunk from that topic.
Phrase as a question when possible.
"How does conref work?" matches user queries better than "Conref Overview".
